diagrams.title: Linear Independence vs Linear Dependence in R²
diagrams.description: Two vectors v1 and v2 are linearly independent if neither can be written as a scalar multiple of the other. In R², any two non-collinear vectors are independent and form a basis. If they lie on the same line, they are dependent and one adds no new 'direction' to the span.
elements: Vector c = (2, 2) shown in red - demonstrating dependent vector on same line as (1,1)
Labels for each vector with coordinates
Dashed lines showing collinearity for dependent vectors
diagrams.purpose: Visualize the fundamental concept of linear independence - two vectors are independent if they point in different directions and neither is a scalar multiple of the other

diagrams.title: Span of Two Vectors in R²
diagrams.description: The span of vectors v1 and v2 is the set of all their linear combinations a*v1 + b*v2. With two independent vectors in R², their span is the entire 2D plane. This demonstrates why any two linearly independent vectors in R² form a basis - they can reach every point in the space.
elements: Two basis vectors: e1 = (1, 0) and e2 = (0, 1)
Grid lines showing linear combinations at integer coefficients
Example: vector (3, 2) as 3*e1 + 2*e2 with arrow from origin to point
Shaded plane/area representing full span coverage
diagrams.purpose: Show that the span of independent basis vectors covers the entire vector space, explaining why a basis 'generates' the space

diagrams.title: Basis and Dimension in R³
diagrams.description: In R³, any set of 3 linearly independent vectors forms a basis. The dimension is 3. This diagram shows three vectors in 3D space that are not coplanar, meaning they point in three distinctly different directions and cannot be expressed as combinations of each other.
elements: Three basis vectors: i = (1, 0, 0), j = (0, 1, 0), k = (0, 0, 1)
Labels for dimension (3) and basis vectors
Visual indication that all three vectors are needed to span 3D space
diagrams.purpose: Extend the concept to R³, showing that dimension equals the number of vectors in any basis, and that all three vectors are necessary to span the full space
